steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A red filter is placed in front of white light. What colour light passes through?
options: All colours except red pass through
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! A colour filter does not add colour — it absorbs (blocks) all colours except its own. A red filter absorbs all wavelengths except red, so only red light passes through.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! A red filter transmits only red light and absorbs all other colours. White light contains all colours, and the filter selectively lets through only red wavelengths.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 6.3.3 — Colour

steps.title: Light & Colour
steps.bodyHTML: White light is made up of all the colours of the visible spectrum. A prism can split white light into a spectrum (dispersion) because each colour has a slightly different wavelength and refracts by a different amount. The order is: Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue, Indigo, Violet (ROYGBIV). Red has the longest wavelength and violet the shortest. Colour filters work by absorbing all colours except the one they transmit. An object appears a certain colour because it reflects that colour and absorbs the rest. A red object in green light appears black because there is no red light to reflect.

relationships: White light = all visible colours combined
Filters absorb all colours except their own
Objects reflect their colour and absorb the rest

commonErrors: Saying a filter adds colour (it absorbs all other colours)
Forgetting that a coloured object in the wrong light appears black
Confusing dispersion (splitting light) with diffraction (bending around obstacles)

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Choose different colour filters and light sources. See which colours pass through and which are absorbed.

aqa.questionText: White light passes through a prism. Explain why a spectrum is formed.

steps.label: State composition
steps.content: White light is made up of different colours (wavelengths) of light.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — white light = mixture of colours/wavelengths

steps.label: Explain refraction
steps.content: Each colour has a different wavelength, so each refracts by a different amount at the prism boundary.

steps.label: Result
steps.content: The colours spread out to form a visible spectrum from red to violet.

aqa.finalAnswer: White light contains different wavelengths that refract by different amounts in the prism, separating into a spectrum.

edexcel.questionText: A red object is viewed under green light. What colour does it appear? Explain why.

steps.label: Explain
steps.content: A red object only reflects red light. Green light contains no red wavelengths, so no light is reflected and the object appears black.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — no red to reflect / all light absorbed

edexcel.finalAnswer: Black — the object absorbs the green light and there is no red light to reflect.

ocr-gateway.questionText: Explain how a blue filter works.

steps.label: What it transmits
steps.content: A blue filter transmits (lets through) blue light.

steps.label: What it absorbs
steps.content: It absorbs all other colours (wavelengths) of light.

ocr-gateway.finalAnswer: A blue filter transmits blue light and absorbs all other colours.

ocr-21c.questionText: Name the process by which a prism splits white light into a spectrum.

steps.label: Brief explanation
steps.content: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts, causing the colours to separate.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — different refraction for different wavelengths

ocr-21c.finalAnswer: Dispersion — different wavelengths refract by different amounts.

wjec.questionText: A white object is viewed under red light. What colour does it appear? Explain.

steps.label: Explain
steps.content: A white object reflects all colours. Under red light, only red light is available to reflect, so it appears red.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — reflects all, only red available

wjec.finalAnswer: Red — a white object reflects all colours, but only red is available.

aqa.questionText: A green object is viewed under white light. What colour does it appear?
markSchemeSteps: Green object reflects green light from the white light ✓

edexcel.questionText: Which colour of visible light has the shortest wavelength?
markSchemeSteps: Violet has the shortest wavelength in the visible spectrum ✓

ocr-gateway.questionText: A blue object is placed under red light. What colour does it appear?
markSchemeSteps: Blue object only reflects blue — no blue in red light → appears black ✓
ocr-gateway.commonMistake: Students sometimes think colours mix (blue + red = purple), but the object can only reflect blue and there is none available.

ocr-21c.questionText: What name is given to the splitting of white light by a prism?
markSchemeSteps: Dispersion is the splitting of white light into a spectrum ✓

wjec.questionText: Which colour in the visible spectrum has the longest wavelength?
markSchemeSteps: Red has the longest wavelength in the visible spectrum ✓

rearrangements.formula: Red has longest wavelength, lowest frequency
rearrangements.label: Red end

rearrangements.formula: Violet has shortest wavelength, highest frequency
rearrangements.label: Violet end

equation.examTip: Remember: filters ABSORB, they do not add colour. A coloured object in the 'wrong' light appears BLACK because there is nothing to reflect.

boardNotes.aqa: Know ROYGBIV order, how filters work, and what happens to coloured objects under different lights.
boardNotes.edexcel: Common question: 'Explain why a red object in blue light appears black.' Use absorb/reflect language.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: Link dispersion to refraction. Each colour has a different wavelength → different refraction.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: Know approximate wavelength range: 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red).
boardNotes.wjec: Describe using 'absorb' and 'reflect/transmit' for full marks on filter questions.
